startingHypothesis.statement: Trust in AI systems is becoming an evidence problem: durable trust will depend less on stated intent and more on independently repeatable assurance signals across evaluation, monitoring, and operational control.
startingHypothesis.rationale: Multiple fresh sources point toward operational evidence, evaluation, and monitoring as core trust mechanisms.

sourcesRead.title: Global Policy Frameworks for Quantum Technologies, Cybersecurity, Climate Change, and Responsible AI Governance
sourcesRead.summary: Abstract This book develops an integrated global policy framework for governing quantum technologies, cybersecurity, climate change and responsible artificial intelligence as interdependent domains of strategic risk, scientific opportunity and institutional transformation. It argues that modern governance cannot treat these domains as separate regulatory silos: quantum advances reshape cryptographic assurance and sensing capacity; cybersecurity determines the reliability of public infrastructures; climate change in

sourcesRead.title: ENTERPRISE MODEL FIREWALL (EMF): A COGNITIVE TRAFFIC CONTROL ARCHITECTURE FOR SECURE AND SCALABLE ENTERPRISE AI SYSTEMS
sourcesRead.venue: International Journal of Generative Artificial Intelligence in Business
sourcesRead.summary: Purpose: This article introduces the Enterprise Model Firewall (EMF) as a novel architectural control layer designed to address the governance, security, and scalability challenges inherent in enterprise generative AI deployments.Problem: Current enterprise AI systems lack unified traffic control mechanisms, exposing organizations to prompt injection attacks, hallucination-driven misinformation, data leakage, and unpredictable operational costs.Method: Through systematic synthesis of over 300 scholarly papers on AI

sourcesRead.title: An Evidence-Based Framework for Patient-Facing Artificial Intelligence Integration in the Emergency Department
sourcesRead.venue: Journal of the American College of Emergency Physicians Open
sourcesRead.summary: The emergency department is a high-need setting for patient-facing artificial intelligence (PF-AI) because it functions as both a health care safety net and, increasingly, a digital front door for unscheduled acute care. We define PF-AI as tools that interact directly with patients to collect information, deliver tailored communication, and support decision making under clinician governance.

sourcesRead.title: INTEGRATION OF INTERNAL AUDIT, ELECTRONIC AUDIT, AND AUDIT GOVERNANCE IN IMPROVING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF INTERNAL CONTROL AND REDUCING THE RISK OF FRAUD
sourcesRead.summary: Digital transformation has increased the complexity of fraud risks within organizations, necessitating a more adaptive and integrated internal control system. This study aims to analyze the integration of internal audit, electronic audit, and audit governance in improving the effectiveness of internal control and mitigating fraud risks.

sourcesRead.title: A Drift-Aware Human-in-the-Loop Edge AI Agent for Wireless IoMT Sensor-Network Intrusion Detection Under Cross-Corpus Shift
sourcesRead.summary: Wireless sensor networks underpin the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), where connected medical devices and wearable body-area sensors stream patient telemetry across hospital networks, and securing this traffic is safety-critical. Machine learning intrusion-detection systems for the IoMT are usually evaluated within a single public dataset, where they report near-perfect detection scores; whether that accuracy predicts performance in a different hospital has not been measured systematically.

sourcesRead.title: Development of a Holistic Assessment Framework for the Design of AI-Based Automation
sourcesRead.summary: There is a need to ensure that the application of artificial intelligence (AI) in increasingly automated operations is safe, human-centric, and trustworthy, and respects ethical principles. To this end, this paper presents an innovative holistic assessment framework to support certification-aware design of AI-based sociotechnical systems with a range of levels of automation along multiple design stages from low to high technology and human readiness levels (TRLs/HRLs).

sourcesRead.title: Wearable and Low-Cost IoT Air Quality Sensing with AI and XAI: A Review of Computational Framework for Personalized Exposure Assessment
sourcesRead.summary: Air pollution monitoring has undergone a significant transformation with the emergence of wearable sensing technologies, low-cost IoT monitoring networks, and AI–driven analytical frameworks. Conventional regulatory monitoring systems, although highly accurate, are spatially sparse and therefore insufficient for capturing the dynamic and heterogeneous nature of individual exposure across diverse microenvironments.

notes.claim: Global Policy Frameworks for Quantum Technologies, Cybersecurity, Climate Change, and Responsible AI Governance adds evidence on assurance measurement.
notes.implication: The trust question should be treated as a testable evidence pipeline, not a static policy claim.
notes.limitation: The metadata-only pass should be followed by closer reading before major confidence changes.

synthesis: Tonight's open-source scan found 7 relevant artifacts clustered around governance, control, audit, framework. The working hypothesis remains viable, but the evidence base is still stronger on evaluation and tooling than on independent, repeatable assurance in deployed systems.

hypothesisUpdates.rationale: Multiple fresh sources point toward operational evidence, evaluation, and monitoring as core trust mechanisms.

newHypotheses.statement: Independent AI assurance will converge around continuously refreshed evidence bundles rather than one-time model reports.
Agentic AI trust failures will be easier to detect through workflow telemetry than through benchmark scores alone.

nextSearchPlan: continuous assurance evidence bundles AI systems
agent workflow telemetry trust monitoring evaluation
independent AI audit benchmark operational assurance
unresolvedQuestions: Which assurance signals remain predictive after deployment conditions drift?
What evidence should be independently reproducible versus vendor-attested?
How should agentic workflows expose trust-relevant telemetry without leaking sensitive data?
